data.title: U.S. futures point lower as oil prices spike amid escalating Iran conflict
data.description:
Investing.com - U.S. stock futures ticked lower on Thursday after oil prices briefly surged back above $100 a barrel, as global moves to release record amounts of strategic crude reserves failed to quell investors anxious about tanker traffic disruptions due to the Iran conflict.
[[pro_promotion |
Track U.S. stock with InvestingPro
]]
By 06:38 ET (10:38 GMT), the Dow futures contract had fallen by 251 points, or 0.5%, S&P 500 futures had fallen by 28 points, or 0.4%, and Nasdaq 100 futures had declined by 92 points, or 0.4%.
Weighing on sentiment has been an effective stoppage to vessels passing through the Strait of Hormuz, a critical shipping choke point surrounded on three sides by Iran. Container companies, keen to protect crews and struggling to find insurance, have all but halted sailings in a narrow waterway that hosts a fifth of the world's oil and liquefied natural gas supply.
Merchant ships in and around the strait have been targeted by strikes, exacerbating fears over squeezed oil flows. On Wednesday, the United Kingdom Maritime Trade Operations, a maritime agency monitoring shipping activity, said a third vessel had be hit by an unknown projectile, after two other ships were hit and left on fire off of Iraq's coast. Iraq and Oman have since moved to close oil terminals.
The International Energy Agency warned that the war in the Middle East is creating the largest supply disruption in the history of the global oil market. The IEA, which previously announced its largest-ever release of strategic oil reserves on Wednesday, slashed its annual supply outlook.
A now more than week-old joint U.S. and Israeli assault on Iran has led to massive fluctuations in prices in recent days and sparking concerns over reigniting inflationary pressures on countries around the world. Bets that central banks, such as the Federal Reserve, will reconsider possible interest rate cuts as pushed up bond yields, further denting the appeal of stocks.
Brent crude was last trading higher by 5.3% at $96.88 a barrel, after having briefly topped $100 a barrel earlier in Asian trading. Earlier this week, the global benchmark soared to as high as nearly $120 a barrel.
On Wednesday, the blue-chip Dow Jones Industrial Average slumped to its lowest closing level so far this year, reflecting worries that the oil shock could hit a range of U.S. businesses and consumers.
Still, the benchmark S&P 500 ended only marginally in the red, while the tech-heavy Nasdaq Composite eked out a gain. Sentiment was bolstered by better-than-expected results from cloud-computing giant Oracle, which featured a rosy forecast for artificial intelligence data center demand. U.S. consumer price data for February also came in as expected, although the outlook for inflation has darkened as observers brace for the impact of the spike in oil prices.
Adobe earnings ahead
Beyond the war in Iran, traders were also eyeing upcoming results from Adobe, curious to see how the Photoshop-maker is grappling with recent concerns around AI's impact on the software sector.
Once thought to be a industry tailwind, the emergence of new AI tools has fueled worries in recent months around potential disruptions that may upend a host of software-as-a-service companies. Investors have particularly been fretting that new flavors of the technology, such as plug-ins to Anthropic's Claude agent that can pour through legal documents, could greatly demand for businesses ranging from data analytics to marketing.
The sector-specific S&P 500 Information Technology, which Adobe is a constituent of, has dropped by more than 3% so far this year. It has been a notable reversal from 2025, when the index notched a total return of 24%.
Shares of Adobe, whose suite of offerings include InDesign and Acrobat, have been reflective of this trend, sliding by more than 17% year-to-date.
Even before this year's existential crisis in software, Adobe had been looking to head off these possible disruptions pursuing its own AI strategy, racing to fold the nascent technology into its services through tools like Firefly and Adobe Express. These enhancements aim to entice users by allowing them to quickly create images and videos within Adobe's Creative Cloud.
The effort to monetize AI developments has appeared to place the company in a solid position, with executives forecasting full-year fiscal 2026 revenue and profit that have topped Wall Street expectations. The firm said it expects annual revenue to between $25.90 billion and $26.10 billion, while earnings per share are seen coming in at a range of $23.30 to $23.50.
Elsewhere, Dollar General shares fell on an annual comparable sales forecast which underwhelmed Wall Street expectations, as the discount retailer struggled to entice bargain-hunting consumers.

data.title: European stocks open lower as oil prices spike amid ongoing Iran conflict
data.description:
Investing.com - European stocks edged lower at the start of trading on Thursday, as oil prices once again briefly jumped above $100 a barrel amid ongoing shipping disruptions caused by the Iran war.
[[pro_promotion |
Track European stocks with InvestingPro
]]
By 04:04 ET (08:04 GMT), the pan-European Stoxx 600 had dipped by 0.4%, the Dax in Germany has dropped 0.2%, the CAC 40 in France had declined 0.5%, and the FTSE 100 in the United Kingdom had fallen 0.5%.
Oil futures spiked, extending a bout of volatility in crude despite efforts by the International Energy Agency to roll out its largest-ever release of strategic oil reserves to help quell the fluctuations.
The U.S. has also said it would tap its own oil reserves, although analysts have flagged that the moves may only provide a temporary respite — and only a reopening of tanker traffic through the vital Strait of Hormuz waterway will offer relief for markets shorn of key supplies.
A fifth of the world's oil supply flows through the narrow choke point south of Iran, but sailings have come to a virtual standstill as Tehran threatens to attack most vessels attempting to traverse the strait.
Reports have suggested that Iran has also laid naval mines, while the U.S. Navy has not committed to escorting ships due to safety concerns.
Traffic through the strait has come to virtual standstill, denting oil flows, driving up crude prices, and fueling fears over a possible spike in inflationary pressures around the globe. Europe, as well as Asia, are particularly big importers of oil and gas that passes through the strait, leaving the regions exposed to the more than week-old U.S. and Israeli assault on Iran.
At 04:05 ET, the Brent futures contract, the global benchmark, had risen by 4.3% to $95.92 a barrel, while U.S. West Texas Intermediate had gained 3.8% to $90.54 per barrel.

data.title: Oil spikes above $100 a barrel; Adobe to report - what's moving markets
data.description:
Investing.com - Futures linked to the main U.S. indices edge down, with investor fretting over intensifying fighting in the Middle East. Oil prices spike once again above the $100 per barrel level, as attacks on shipping vessels near a crucial waterway south of Iran fuel concerns over continued supply disruptions. Gold steadies, but remains under pressure from inflation worries caused by the oil shock. Adobe is due to unveil its latest earnings, while oil major Shell also reports.
1. Futures slip
U.S. stock futures pointed lower on Thursday, after oil prices rose back above $100 per barrel, despite efforts to release massive crude reserves to help counter the disruptions stemming from the war in Iran.
By 04:10 ET (08:10 GMT), the Dow futures contract had fallen by 218 points, or 0.5%, S&P 500 had dropped by 25 points, or 0.4%, and Nasdaq 100 futures had slipped by 93 points, or 0.4%.
On Wednesday, the blue-chip Dow Jones Industrial Average slumped to its lowest closing level so far this year, reflecting worries that the oil shock could hit a range of U.S. businesses and consumers.
Still, the benchmark S&P 500 ended only marginally in the red, while the tech-heavy Nasdaq Composite eked out a gain. Sentiment was bolstered by better-than-expected results from cloud-computing giant Oracle, which featured a rosy forecast for artificial intelligence data center demand. U.S. consumer price data for February also came in as expected, although the outlook for inflation has darkened as observers brace for the impact of the spike in oil prices.
Although the effects of the more than week-old joint U.S.-Israeli assault on Iran remained the major focus for markets, other themes have continued to percolate in the background, including anxiety in the private-credit industry, continued uncertainty around the trajectory of U.S. tariffs, and worries around the returns from huge AI spending.
2. Oil tops $100 a barrel
Oil prices briefly rose back above the key $100 per barrel, with fears over disrupted oil flows showing few signs of abating as an escalating Iran conflict continues to engulf much of the Middle East.
At 04:05 ET, the Brent futures contract, the global benchmark, had climbed by 4.3% to $95.92 a barrel, while U.S. West Texas Intermediate had gained 3.8% to $90.54 per barrel.
Crude prices have swung wildly in recent days, underscoring how sensitive investors have become to the constantly-evolving developments in the Iran conflict. At one point earlier this week, Brent had soared to nearly $120 a barrel, its highest mark since 2022.
For oil markets, the core concern has revolved around a stoppage to flows through the Strait of Hormuz, the narrow waterway south of Iran that hosts a fifth of the world's oil and gas supplies - much of it destined for Asia and Europe.
Tanker traffic has come to a virtual standstill as the threat of Iranian attacks fuels worries over the safety of crews. Shipping companies have struggled to receive insurance for the perilous sailings as well, furthering weighing on crossings.
Iran has moved to step up its attacks, while the U.S. Navy has declined to escort ships through the strait. At least six ships have been hit in the last day, while Bahrain said its oil facilities had been targeted.
All of this comes despite the International Energy Agency unveiling its largest-ever release of emergency oil reserves, a decision aimed at quelling fluctuating oil markets. The U.S. Energy Department also said it would release 172 million barrels of oil from its own strategic reserves.
3. Gold steadies
Gold prices stabilized, after falling in Asian trade, as few signs of de-escalation in the U.S.-Israel war with Iran spurred flows into higher energy prices stoked inflation fears.
Spot gold edged up 0.1% to $5,178.65/oz by 04:54 ET, while gold futures ticked up 0.1% to $5,184.75/oz.
While bullion continued to flit between the $5,000-$5,200 an ounce level, pressurized by the oil shock, which some analysts fret could reignite price growth. Central banks, such as the Federal Reserve, may be forced to reconsider near-term interest rate cuts as a result, leading to a firmer dollar.
A stronger greenback can dent gold, making the yellow metal more expensive for overseas buyers and taking the sheen off of its traditional safe-haven appeal. The dollar index last rose roughly 0.2%, close to a two-month high.
4. Adobe to report
Adobe is set to report its latest results after the closing bell on Thursday, with investors curious to see how the Photoshop-maker is grappling with recent concerns around artificial intelligence's impact on the software sector.
Once thought to be a industry tailwind, the emergence of new AI tools has fueled worries in recent months around potential disruptions that may upend a host of software-as-a-service companies. Investors have particularly been fretting that new flavors of the technology, such as plug-ins to Anthropic's Claude agent that can pour through legal documents, could greatly demand for businesses ranging from data analytics to marketing.
The sector-specific S&P 500 Information Technology, which Adobe is a constituent of, has dropped by more than 3% so far this year. It has been a notable reversal from 2025, when the index notched a total return of 24%.
Shares of Adobe, whose suite of offerings include InDesign and Acrobat, have been reflective of this trend, sliding by more than 18% year-to-date.
Even before this year's existential crisis in software, Adobe had been looking to head off these possible disruptions pursuing its own AI strategy, racing to fold the nascent technology into its services through tools like Firefly and Adobe Express. These enhancements aim to entice users by allowing them to quickly create images and videos within Adobe's Creative Cloud.
The effort to monetize AI developments has appeared to place the company in a solid position, with executives forecasting full-year fiscal 2026 revenue and profit that has topped Wall Street expectations. The firm said it expects annual revenue to between $25.90 billion and $26.10 billion, while earnings per share are seen coming in at a range of $23.30 to $23.50.
5. Shell earnings
Oil major Shell reported adjusted earnings of $18.5 billion in 2025, down from $23.7 billion in 2024.
Cash flow from operating activities reached $42.9 billion, compared with $54.7 billion the previous year. Free cash flow amounted to $26.1 billion, down from $39.5 billion in 2024.
Shell maintained significant shareholder distributions during the year. Total distributions reached about $22.4 billion, including $8.5 billion in dividends and $13.9 billion in share buybacks, representing roughly 52% of cash flow from operations and placing payouts at the top end of the company's 40%–50% target range.
The results come a day after Reuters reported that Shell, the world's largest liquefied natural gas trader, declared force majeure on LNG cargoes it purchases from QatarEnergy and sells to customers globally after Qatar halted production at its 77 million-tonne-per-year LNG facility and declared force majeure on shipments.
Analysts estimate Shell takes about 6.8 million tonnes per annum of Qatari LNG under supply arrangements, while TotalEnergies is estimated to take around 5.2 mtpa, the report said.
